The example shows a failed query with `data: null`, and describes key error fields such as `message`, `locations`, `path`, and `extensions.classification`. It also mentions that the server adds error-specific information to `extensions`, and introduces the distinction between client and internal errors.

children.title: Structure of the error response
Client errors
children.summary: Explains GraphQL error handling for client-side issues in the REST application. Even when a query fails, GraphQL returns HTTP 200 with an errors array plus data, where failed queries may be null and successful ones still returned. The error object includes message, locations, path, and extensions/classification; the path helps identify which query failed in multi-query requests. Notes that messages are for debugging and should not be shown on the UI. Also mentions accept-language defaults and priority behavior.

children.title: Internal errors
children.summary: This section explains how REST API errors are classified as internal errors versus client errors. It states that any error not treated as a client error is internal, the cause is not shown to the user, and all such errors use code -150600. It also contrasts this with client errors, which may carry localized messages and identifiers from AppException or ConversionException, and notes that detailed tracing is only logged at trace_on or higher.

Explains GraphQL and REST error-response structure. GraphQL always returns HTTP 200 with separate data and errors objects; failed queries may be null while successful ones still return, and error fields include message, locations, path, and extensions/classification. The path identifies the failing query in combined requests, and messages are for debugging only. For REST client errors, extensions include client_error, code (-130002 or -150601), and localized error_messages from AppException or ConversionException. All other errors are internal, hidden from users, and use code -150600.

Explains GraphQL error handling and response format. GraphQL returns HTTP 200 even when some queries fail, with errors reported in an `errors` array alongside `data`. The section covers key fields (`message`, `locations`, `path`, `extensions.classification`) and notes `extensions` may include server-specific details. It then distinguishes client errors from internal errors: client errors include localized messages, `client_error: true`, and codes `-130002` or `-150601`; internal errors hide the cause and use code `-150600`.

Explains how to call the REST-integrated GraphQL endpoint (`POST https://<server>:<port>/Rest/graphql`) by inspecting the schema, building a query body, and requesting only needed fields. Shows example queries for `readIntegratedCase` and `readPerson`. Covers access control via authentication and facade SIDs, plus CORS/CSRF protections and response headers. Also describes localization via the `accept-language` header. Error handling uses HTTP 200 with `errors` plus `data`, and distinguishes client errors (localized, `client_error: true`, codes `-130002`/`-150601`) from internal errors (`-150600`).

Defines GraphQL in Social Program Management as a schema-based API query language returning JSON over POST `/Rest/graphql`, deployed inside the REST EAR. Covers core terms, endpoint enablement, introspection, depth/complexity limits, and admin property setup. Explains building GraphQL APIs with schema files, Java data fetchers, and runtime wiring; schema merging/validation; and performance considerations. Also describes GraphiQL use in dev only, client query examples, security (auth, SIDs, CORS/CSRF), localization, and error handling with client vs internal errors.

Explains how to develop custom inbound Cúram REST APIs when existing APIs are insufficient. Covers REST design basics and conventions (plural lowercase paths, snake_case, collection/member resources, consistent JSON representations, no HATEOAS), gap analysis before modeling, and a Notes API example. Describes modeling JSON resources with Cúram structs/domains, creating and aggregating facades/operations, and following the build/deploy/test process. Also includes REST infrastructure topics such as ResourcesConfig/Swagger, security, CORS/CSRF, error handling, runtime enablement/disablement, and GraphQL API support, client usage, security, localization, and error handling.

children.title: 1.3 Developing outbound REST APIs
Before you begin
children.summary: Outbound REST APIs must use JSON. To create one in Social Program Management, you need to write Java classes, optionally store authentication credentials in the Properties table, and manually add required third-party JARs to the application server and class path. Outbound API code goes under the SERVER_DIR custom folder (typically EJBServer/components/custom) and is packaged into Curam.ear, not Rest.ear. Ant build targets include the new classes automatically; no extra configuration is needed.

children.title: Getting started
Storing Java classes
children.summary: Explains how to add outbound REST API Java code to Social Program Management. Outbound API classes go under the SERVER_DIR custom folder (typically EJBServer/components/custom) and are packaged into Curam.ear, not Rest.ear. Shows the typical package hierarchy for client configuration, API classes, and optional serializers, mappers, modules, and request filters. Also notes that authentication credentials must be stored in the Properties table with encryption, and third-party JARs must be added manually to the EAR and class path.

children.title: Storing authentication credentials
children.summary: Explains how to securely store authentication credentials for outbound REST API integrations. If the API provider requires authentication, add new fields to the Properties database table and encrypt any passwords before storing them. The section sits within guidance for building outbound APIs into the Cúram EAR and references related authentication details in “Authenticating with the API service.”

children.title: Building outbound APIs into the Cúram EAR
children.summary: Explains how to add outbound REST API support to the Cúram EAR. Requires JSON-based APIs, writing Java classes under EJBServer/components/custom, optionally adding serializer/mapper/module/filter classes, and storing encrypted credentials in the Properties table if authentication is needed. Outbound APIs are packaged into Curam.ear, not Rest.ear. Notes that build targets automatically include new Java classes, but required third-party JARs must be manually added to the EAR and application server class path.

Introductory guidance for building outbound REST APIs in Social Program Management. It says the API must use JSON, requires Java classes under SERVER_DIR/custom and packaging into Curam.ear (not Rest.ear), and may include optional serializer/mapper/module/filter classes. If authentication is needed, credentials must be stored in the Properties table and encrypted. It also notes that build targets include new Java classes automatically, but third-party JARs must be added manually to the EAR and class path.

children.title: Serializing JSON and Java objects
Creating custom serialization
Creating custom serializer and deserializer classes
children.summary: Explains how to create custom Jackson serializer and deserializer classes by extending `StdSerializer` or `JsonDeserializer` and registering them on a custom `ObjectMapper`. Notes that registering for a Java type affects all attributes of that type in JSON mapping. Provides an example `CustomDateSerializer` for Cúram `Date` values that outputs ISO8601 strings, handles `Date.kZeroDate` as null, and implements `ContextualSerializer`.

children.title: Registering the custom classes with Jackson
children.summary: Explains that custom Jackson serializers/deserializers must be registered to override default handling. Shows creating a `SimpleModule` subclass (`CustomSimpleModule`) that adds a custom serializer and deserializer for `curam.util.type.Date`, including a date deserializer that maps empty/invalid ISO8601 (`yyyy-MM-dd`) strings to `Date.kZeroDate`. This section is about wiring custom date handling into Jackson via module registration.

Explains how to customize Jackson serialization by creating custom `StdSerializer`/`JsonDeserializer` classes and registering them on a custom `ObjectMapper`. Emphasizes that type-based registration affects all fields of that Java type. Shows a `CustomDateSerializer` for Cúram `Date` values that outputs ISO8601 (`yyyy-MM-dd`) and treats `Date.kZeroDate` as null, plus a `CustomDateDeserializer` that maps empty or invalid date strings to `Date.kZeroDate`. Also demonstrates wiring both into Jackson via a `SimpleModule`.

Explains how Jersey REST client JSON mapping works with Jackson POJOs and default type mappings, and when custom serialization is needed. Shows that nested JSON/arrays must map to complex Java objects/lists. Introduces custom Jackson `StdSerializer`/`JsonDeserializer` classes for types like Cúram `Date`, noting type-based registration affects all fields of that type. Gives ISO8601 `yyyy-MM-dd` serializer/deserializer examples that treat `Date.kZeroDate`, empty strings, invalid dates, and nulls as empty/default values, then registers them via a `SimpleModule` and custom `ObjectMapper`.

children.title: Creating the Jersey REST client
Configuring the REST client
children.summary: This section explains how to configure a Jersey REST client before creating the client instance. It shows registering custom providers such as a custom ObjectMapper provider when using custom Jackson serializer/deserializer classes, and setting client properties like read timeout and connection timeout. It also notes that custom providers are only needed when replacing Jackson’s default serializers/deserializers.

children.title: Creating the REST client
children.summary: Explains how to create a Jersey REST client using a reusable ClientConfig, emphasizing that client creation/disposal is heavyweight and the same client instance can be reused for multiple outbound requests. Shows creating a client with ClientBuilder.newClient(clientConfig), optionally adding request filters, and provides a full example where ClientConfig registers a custom ObjectMapper provider and sets read/connect timeouts. Also notes the default transport is HttpUrlConnection and alternative connectors like Apache require extra Jersey libraries.

children.title: Full example of how to configure and create a REST client
children.summary: Shows a complete Jersey REST client setup example. It explains that one shared ClientConfig can be used for all outbound API requests when the same ObjectMapper, serializers, and deserializers apply. It notes Jersey’s default HttpUrlConnection transport and mentions alternative connectors like ApacheConnectorProvider require extra libraries. The example code creates a ClientConfig, registers a custom ObjectMapper provider, sets read and connection timeouts, and builds a Client with ClientBuilder.newClient(...).

Explains how to configure and create a Jersey REST client for outbound API requests. It shows registering a custom ObjectMapper/provider only when using custom Jackson serializers/deserializers, setting properties such as read and connect timeouts, and reusing one ClientConfig across requests. It notes Jersey’s default HttpUrlConnection transport, mentions optional alternative connectors like Apache, and emphasizes that client creation/disposal is heavyweight, so a single Client can be reused. Includes a full example using ClientBuilder.newClient(clientConfig).

children.title: Making an outbound API request that uses the REST client
Constructing an outbound request
children.summary: Explains how to build and send an outbound REST API request with Jersey. It covers creating a `WebTarget` with URL, path, and query parameters; adding headers and expected response media type via `Invocation.Builder`; and invoking GET, POST, or PUT requests, including sending a JSON body with a POJO. It also notes checking the HTTP response code and deserializing a successful response body to a Java object.

children.title: Sending an outbound request
children.summary: Explains how to send an outbound REST request using a Jersey client. Covers building a `WebTarget` with URL, path, and query parameters, then adding headers and expected response media type with `Invocation.Builder`. Shows how to execute GET requests with `.get()` and POST/PUT requests with an entity body. Also notes checking the HTTP status code and deserializing a successful response body into a Java object.

children.title: Handling the response from the request
children.summary: Explains how to handle Jersey REST client responses: check the HTTP status before deserializing, since success codes vary by API (e.g., 200, 201, 204). If successful and a body exists, use `readEntity()` to map JSON to a Java POJO; if not, close the response manually unless `readEntity()` was called. Error responses may be handled by status code alone or deserialized into an error POJO. Notes that `readEntity()` closes the connection and that generic `Object.class` can map JSON to a `Map`.

children.title: Full example of how to make an outbound API request
children.summary: Shows a complete Java REST client example for an outbound GET request: building a WebTarget with path/query params, adding an Authorization header, sending the request, reading a successful 200 response, handling 401 reauthentication, and closing failed responses. It then explains API authentication as a two-step process: obtain an access token from an auth URL via POST with credentials, then include the token in outbound requests until it expires. Includes a note on securely storing credentials and an example token JSON response.

Shows how to use the Jersey REST client to make outbound API calls and process responses. It covers building a WebTarget with URL, path, and query parameters; adding headers and expected media type with Invocation.Builder; sending GET, POST, and PUT requests, including JSON request bodies via POJOs; and checking status codes before deserializing response entities. It also explains handling success and error responses, closing connections properly, and using readEntity() or Object.class for JSON mapping.

children.title: Authenticating with the API service
Authenticating and retrieving an access token
children.summary: Explains how to authenticate to an API service and retrieve an access token via a programmatic POST request using the REST client. Describes the typical two-step flow: call an authentication URL with credentials, receive a token, then include that token in subsequent outbound requests until it expires. Includes a security note to store credentials securely (for example, encrypt passwords in the Properties table and decrypt before use) and shows an example token response with access_token and token_type.

children.title: Sending an access token in the outbound API request
children.summary: Explains how to send an access token with outbound API requests, typically using an Authorization header with a Bearer token. Notes that access tokens and optional refresh tokens should be retained temporarily, with 401/invalid_token responses indicating expiration and prompting a refresh-token request or reauthentication when needed. Also covers building and deploying outbound APIs with Ant, including adding required Jersey JARs to the EJBServer class path and custom lib directory for the EAR.

children.title: Refresh tokens
children.summary: Explains how to handle OAuth-style refresh tokens for outbound API calls. It notes that access tokens must be retained and sent in request headers, often using a Bearer token. If the API returns a refresh token and expiry, store it temporarily too. When a 401/invalid_token response indicates expiration, use the refresh token to request a new access token; when the refresh token itself expires, resend credentials. Also begins guidance on building and deploying outbound APIs and required Jersey JAR locations.

Explains how to authenticate with an API service before making outbound REST calls. Describes the typical two-step flow: POST credentials to an authentication URL, receive an access token (example JSON includes access_token and token_type), then send that token with subsequent requests until it expires. Includes a security note to store credentials securely, such as encrypting passwords in the Properties table and decrypting them before use.

children.title: Building and deploying outbound APIs
Building the outbound REST APIs in your development environment
children.summary: Explains how to build and deploy outbound REST APIs in the development environment. It lists the required Jersey/HK2/Jackson/Javassist JARs and their locations, then gives setup steps for Eclipse (set J2EE_JAR classpath variable, add external JARs) and for Ant builds (set J2EE_JAR env var, copy JARs into EJBServer/components/custom/lib, use standard server and EAR targets). It also notes deployment is normal, with a class-loading fix: remove jsr311-api-1.0.jar if javax.ws.rs.core.Response conflicts occur.

children.title: Building the application by using Ant targets
children.summary: Explains how to build outbound REST APIs with Ant targets. You must set the J2EE_JAR environment variable, copy the required Jersey JARs into EJBServer/components/custom/lib so they’re added to the compile classpath and Cúram EAR, then use the standard server target to compile. Use websphereEAR or weblogicEAR to package the APIs into Curam.ear. Notes deployment is standard, but if a javax.ws.rs.core.Response class-loading conflict occurs, remove jsr311-api-1.0.jar from the EAR.

children.title: Deploying to an application server
children.summary: Explains how to deploy the built Cúram EAR to an application server and notes a runtime class-loading issue to watch for. If `javax.ws.rs.core.Response` is already loaded, remove `jsr311-api-1.0.jar` from the EAR because its JAX-RS 1.0 APIs can conflict with server-provided JAX-RS classes. This section is a short deployment note following the build instructions for outbound REST APIs.

children.title: Configuring a WebSphere application server to use Jersey
children.summary: Explains how to configure WebSphere Application Server 9+ to use the Jersey JAX-RS implementation packaged in the Cúram EAR instead of WebSphere’s default Apache CXF. The key step is setting the WebSphere JAX-RS provider to support only the JAX-RS 2.0 specification. Also includes troubleshooting for adding REST JARs, noting when javax.ws.rs-api-2.0.jar is needed, classpath issues with J2EE_JAR in Eclipse, and extra JARs required for older Java EE 6 application servers.

children.title: Troubleshooting when adding JARs to your application server or Tomcat environment
children.summary: Troubleshoots JAR-related issues when deploying to application servers or Tomcat. Explains that javax.ws.rs-api-2.0.jar is usually already provided, but should be added if javax.ws.rs.* classes are missing, and that the J2EE_JAR Eclipse classpath must point to an up-to-date Java EE JAR. Notes older Java EE 6 servers may need extra JARs and can conflict with JAX-RS 1.0. Also covers configuring WebSphere 9+ to use Jersey by setting the JAX-RS provider to 2.0 instead of Apache CXF.

Explains how to build and deploy outbound REST APIs into the Cúram EAR. It lists the required Jersey-related JARs and where to place them for Eclipse development and Ant builds, including J2EE_JAR setup and copying JARs into EJBServer/components/custom/lib. Deployment is standard, but a javax.ws.rs.core.Response class-loading conflict may require removing jsr311-api-1.0.jar. Also covers configuring WebSphere 9+ to use Jersey instead of CXF and troubleshooting missing JAX-RS/JAR issues, especially for older Java EE 6 servers.

children.title: Adding client request filters
Creating a client request filter
children.summary: Explains how to add a client request filter as a hook for processing all outbound REST requests. Shows that a filter implements `ClientRequestFilter`, can modify request headers such as adding `Accept-Language: en-US`, and is registered with the client using `client.register(...)` so it applies to every request. Also notes that HTTPS/SSL is handled by the application server, and warns that self-signed certificates may cause `SSLHandshakeException`; accepting them is only for test/development, not production.

children.title: Registering a client request filter with a client
children.summary: Explains how to add a client request filter to hook into all outbound REST requests and apply shared processing. Shows creating a `ClientRequestFilter` implementation, such as adding an `Accept-Language: en-US` header in `filter(ClientRequestContext)`, and registering it with a client via `client.register(new OutboundRequestFilter())`. Also notes HTTPS/SSL is handled by the application server and warns that self-signed certificates may cause `SSLHandshakeException`, with nonproduction workaround guidance.

Explains how to add a client request filter as a hook for all outbound REST requests. Shows implementing `ClientRequestFilter` to centralize request processing, such as adding an `Accept-Language: en-US` header in `filter(ClientRequestContext)`, and registering it with `client.register(...)` so it applies to every request. Notes HTTPS/SSL is handled by the application server and warns that self-signed certificates may trigger `SSLHandshakeException`; accepting them is for test/development only, not production.

children.title: Communicating over HTTPS/SSL
children.summary: Explains how outbound REST requests can use a ClientRequestFilter to apply shared request logic, such as adding an Accept-Language header, by registering the filter with the client. Also notes that HTTPS/SSL is handled by the application server, and that self-signed certificates may cause SSLHandshakeException; Jersey can be configured to trust them in test/development only, not production. The rest of the pages are legal notices, copyright, trademarks, and sample-code/disclaimer information.

Cúram outbound REST APIs use the Jersey client to call external JSON-based services. This section covers setup and packaging in EJBServer/components/custom into Curam.ear, optional auth credentials in the Properties table, and manual addition of required Jersey/third-party JARs. It explains POJO/JSON mapping, custom Jackson serializers/deserializers and ObjectMapper registration, creating and reusing a Jersey Client, making GET/POST/PUT requests, handling responses, authenticating with access tokens, deploying/troubleshooting JAR conflicts, and adding request filters/SSL considerations.

Cúram supports both inbound and outbound REST integration. This section introduces existing Social Program/Child Welfare APIs, Swagger discovery, and basic REST usage (collection/member GETs, field filtering, POST with JSON, auth/session and error handling). It also covers creating custom inbound APIs when gaps exist, including REST design conventions, JSON resource modeling, facades/operations, build/deploy/test, CORS/CSRF, and GraphQL support. Outbound integration uses Jersey clients to call external JSON services, with setup, JSON mapping, auth, JAR/deployment conflicts, request filters, and SSL considerations.
